text: The surgeon identifies the Western perineal artery, highlighting its location and orientation within the surgical field.
The thoracic muscle is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the external arteriomaniac, highlighting its anatomical location.
The surgeon identifies the anatomical landmarks, including the inner iliac artery, external iliac artery, and internal iliac artery, in proximity to the uterus.
The surgeon identifies the uterus and points out the internal iliac artery located in front of it, noting its position and initial dislocation.
The surgeon visually identifies the uterus, the pararectal space, and the uterine artery within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a single artery with a bifurcation located in front of it.
The surgeon exposes a very deep uterine vein, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery and notes its position relative to the uterine artery, which is located behind it. The ureter is visually confirmed and highlighted in the surgical field.

The surgeon increases the space in the surgical field to improve visibility, focusing on the area in front of the ureter where the false pararectal space is located. The ureter is identified and manipulated for better access.

The surgeon identifies and manipulates the ureter, developing space in front of it. The ureter is positioned on the base, with the pararectal space visible nearby. The surgeon proceeds to open the paravesical space.
The surgeon uses a paralytic force to grasp the tissue, demonstrating how the fabric does not stretch when tension is applied.
The surgeon identifies the rectal artery and begins to separate it from the adjacent vein, using precise dissection techniques to distinguish between the two vascular structures.
The surgeon gently disperses tissue without using energy devices, aiming to minimize risk to the endometrial cavity.

The surgeon gently pulls on the tissue without breaking it, ensuring proper confrontation without excessive tightening. The tissue appears ischemic, showing a pale white color, indicating effective tightening.

The surgeon adjusts the technique for holding the suture thread, demonstrating a change in orientation while maneuvering the uterus upwards. The needle remains consistent, and the surgeon moves back slightly, positioning away from the trunk before proceeding with the next step.
